data.html:
"""Centralized configuration for the news editor agent."""

from __future__ import annotations

import os
from datetime import date, datetime, time, timedelta, timezone

# Model list for Executive Editor parallel judgments.
EE_MODELS = [
    "google/gemini-2.5-flash-lite",
    "openai/gpt-5-nano",
    "google/gemini-3-flash-preview"
]

# Model for the Editor-in-Chief synthesis.
EIC_MODEL = "google/gemini-3-pro-preview"

# RSS feed sources for parsing.
FEED_URLS = [
    "https://techcrunch.com/category/artificial-intelligence/feed/",
    "https://www.theverge.com/rss/ai-artificial-intelligence/index.xml",
    "https://www.wired.com/feed/tag/ai/latest/rss",
    "https://www.engadget.com/rss.xml",
    "https://arstechnica.com/ai/feed/",
    "https://feed.cnet.com/feed/topics/ai"

]

# Maximum number of items to process per feed fetch.
MAX_ITEMS_PER_FEED = 50

# Adjust this constant to widen or narrow the ingestion window.
DAYS_BACK = 7

# Stage 3 language for the final narrative.
STAGE3_LANGUAGE = "English"

# Stage prompts are defined as module-level constants for transparency and reuse.
STAGE1_PROMPT = """
You are an Executive Editor at a newsroom covering AI tech. You are preparing concise and factual news digest for readers of the newsroom focusing on product launches in consumer AI. You are given the article payload constructed from the article records and, for each article, includes article index, title, and full article text. 

Follow the instructions below for news-picking:
- Only use the articles that exist in the article payload. Do not fabricate articles.
- Use the provided batch of articles to identify the most newsworthy product launches in consumer AI. Ensure relevance to the reader profile.
- Prioritize breadth + impact: launches that are new, widely available, or represent a clear step forward in usability, technology, or capabilities.
- Ignore rumors, leaks, or roadmap statements without a shipped or announced product.

Follow the instructions below on writing style:
- Follow “what launched, why it matters, and who it helps” packed in a TL;DR format. Avoid classy summary prose.
- Point to details when available (pricing/tiers, availability (locations, waitlist, beta, platforms)), use factual & technical terms. Avoid hype or marketing language; no metaphors; no creativity. 
- Assume readers are informed but time-constrained; optimize for scanability and fast comprehension.

Here are a few examples of the news TL;DR items:

➣ **Marshall launches Heddon multi-room audio hub**. The $300 device adds multi-room streaming to existing Bluetooth speakers via Auracast, supporting Spotify Connect, AirPlay, and Google Cast.

➣ **Google introduces Universal Commerce Protocol for AI agents**. New AI agent shopping standard designed to streamline automated purchasing and product discovery for consumer-facing AI assistants; now in early adoption.

➣ **Google Veo 3.1 model updated for YouTube creators**. Generates native vertical videos from reference images with improved fidelity and 4K upscaling; integrated into YouTube creator tools.

➣ **OpenAI launches ChatGPT Health**. OpenAI launched ChatGPT Health, a dedicated tab for medical inquiries allowing users to connect health records and wellness apps for physician-informed AI analysis with privacy protections. Available globally on Web/iOS/Android.

Here's a summary of newsroom reader profile: Readers are tech-savvy professionals who follow AI as a mainstream consumer technology rather than an academic field. They regularly use products like AI assistants, creative tools, productivity apps, and smart devices, and want to know what is newly available, what changed, and whether it is worth trying or switching. They value concise, factual reporting with clear signals on availability, pricing, platforms, and real-world usefulness, and are skeptical of hype, vague claims, and long-term speculation.

Output format (strict):
- Return top 10–12 TL;DR items. Each TL;DR item is exactly 1 line:
- Use Markdown bold ONLY for the News Title. The News Description must be plain text.
- The period after the bold title is required. Format must be: ➣ **News Title**. News Description
- Start the description immediately after the period; do NOT write “TL;DR”, “Summary:”, dashes, or prefixes.
Line 1: ➣ **News Title**. News Description
- Put ONE blank line between items. No headings, bullets, numbering, or extra text.

"""

STAGE2_PROMPT = """
You are an Executive Editor at a newsroom covering AI tech. You are ranking several news digests using the defined editorial criteria to identify the strongest coverage of product launches in consumer AI. You are given the anonymized news digest drafts from other Executive Editors colleagues.  

Evaluate each response individually. Rank all responses from best to worst. Follow the editorial (evaluation) criteria for rankings:
- Articles relevance to product launches in consumer AI. Articles relevance to reader profile.
- News value and significance based on product/service availability, prominence, authoritativeness, freshness and usability of content.
- Writing style is concise TL;DR execution that points to concrete details; avoids hype or marketing language.
- Assume readers are informed but time-constrained; optimize for scanability and fast comprehension.
- If any response is empty or unusable, rank it last.

Here's a summary of newsroom reader profile: Readers are tech-savvy professionals who follow AI as a mainstream consumer technology rather than an academic field. They regularly use products like AI assistants, creative tools, productivity apps, and smart devices, and want to know what is newly available, what changed, and whether it is worth trying or switching. They value concise, factual reporting with clear signals on availability, pricing, platforms, and real-world usefulness, and are skeptical of hype, vague claims, and long-term speculation.

Output format (strict):
- Return the ranking list. No additional text after the ranking section. Use only the anonymized labels provided.
FINAL RANKING:
1. Response C
2. Response A
3. Response B
"""

STAGE3_PROMPT = """
You are an Editor in Chief at a newsroom covering AI tech. You are performing editorial judgment to prepare a single ready-for-production news digest for readers of the newsroom focusing on product launches in consumer AI. You are given the news digest drafts and their rankings from other Executive Editors colleagues.

Follow the judgment instructions below:
- Consider the peer rankings and what they reveal about response quality.
- Consider any patterns of agreement or disagreement.
- It’s allowed to synthesize individual news items from different news digest drafts in the single ready-for-production news digest. 
- Delete news item duplicates if detected.
- Aim to represent the Executive Editors and Editor in Chief council's collective wisdom.
- Do not modify the wording of individual news items provided by Executive Editors, except to normalize formatting to match the output rules below.

Here's a summary of newsroom reader profile: Readers are tech-savvy professionals who follow AI as a mainstream consumer technology rather than an academic field. They regularly use products like AI assistants, creative tools, productivity apps, and smart devices, and want to know what is newly available, what changed, and whether it is worth trying or switching. They value concise, factual reporting with clear signals on availability, pricing, platforms, and real-world usefulness, and are skeptical of hype, vague claims, and long-term speculation.

Output format (strict):
- Return top 10–12 TL;DR items. Each TL;DR item is exactly 1 line:
- Use Markdown bold ONLY for the News Title. The News Description must be plain text (no bold/italic/links/quotes).
- The period after the bold title is required. Format must be: ➣ **News Title**. News Description
- Start the description immediately after the period; do NOT write “TL;DR”, “Summary:”, dashes, or prefixes.
Line 1: ➣ **News Title**. News Description
- Put ONE blank line between items. No headings, bullets, numbering, or extra text.
"""

def _parse_model_list(raw_value: str | None) -> list[str]:
    """Parse a comma-separated model list, trimming whitespace."""

    if not raw_value:
        return []
    return [item.strip() for item in raw_value.split(",") if item.strip()]


def get_ee_models() -> list[str]:
    """Return the Executive Editor model list, honoring env overrides."""

    override = _parse_model_list(os.getenv("EE_MODELS"))
    return override or EE_MODELS


def get_eic_model() -> str:
    """Return the Editor-in-Chief model name, honoring env overrides."""

    override = os.getenv("EIC_MODEL", "").strip()
    return override or EIC_MODEL


def date_window(reference_date: date | None = None) -> tuple[datetime, datetime]:
    """Return the inclusive datetime window for accepted publication dates."""

    today = reference_date or date.today()
    # Include today plus the previous DAYS_BACK - 1 calendar days.
    start_date = today - timedelta(days=DAYS_BACK - 1)
    start_dt = datetime.combine(start_date, time.min, tzinfo=timezone.utc)
    end_dt = datetime.combine(today, time.max, tzinfo=timezone.utc)
    return start_dt, end_dt


def is_within_range(published_at: datetime, reference_date: date | None = None) -> bool:
    """Return True if the given datetime is within the allowed window."""

    start_dt, end_dt = date_window(reference_date)
    return start_dt <= published_at <= end_dt
